reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Throughput modes section is exemplary — comparison table, per-variant H3 with code example for provisioned, and an opinionated 'start with elastic' recommendation that gives the reader a clear path.

Backups section correctly implements the 'when to enable / when default is fine / tradeoff / opinion' decision-support pattern required by the quality discipline — one of the stronger executions of this pattern in the draft.

FAQ covers exactly 10 questions across all three required categories (Stacktape-specific: how to mount, Lambda compatibility, throughput choice; AWS service use-case: cost, multi-container access, encryption, size limits, backup procedure; comparison: EFS vs S3, EFS vs EBS).

Mounting to containers section fills the most critical practical gap — shows actual volumeMounts configuration with a realistic two-resource example, explains the multi-replica sharing behavior, and lists all four supporting workload types.

When NOT to use comparison table is immediately actionable with concrete alternatives for each use case, and the 10× cost comparison (EFS vs S3) gives the reader a concrete signal rather than vague guidance.

All code examples use correct class names from the approved whitelist (EfsFilesystem, WebService, StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging) and properly hide import lines with focus markers while keeping them in the source for type-check correctness.
ApiReference definitionName exactly matches the source type (EfsFilesystemProps), placed at the correct bottom position matching the example page.
problems: The rootDirectory property appears in the volumeMounts table as a documented option but no code example demonstrates it, leaving users without a concrete pattern for the subdirectory-per-workload use case described in the surrounding prose.

EFS storage cost (~$0.30/GB/month) is stated twice — once in the pricing line after the opening paragraph and again in the When NOT to use cost comparison — creating minor redundancy without adding new information on the second mention.

The Throughput modes table uses a standalone 'Default' column (Yes/No) which is a slightly awkward pattern not seen in the example page's tables; the default could be marked more naturally inline (e.g., appending '(default)' to the mode name in the Mode column).
optionalImprovements: Add a second volumeMounts code example or extend the existing one with rootDirectory set (e.g., rootDirectory: '/service-a') to demonstrate the per-workload directory isolation pattern described in prose.

Remove the cost figure from the 'When NOT to use' section or make the second mention additive — for example, expand it to include the elastic per-GiB transfer cost that isn't mentioned in the intro line, so the second reference earns its place.

In the Throughput modes comparison table, replace the separate 'Default' column with an inline annotation on the mode name row — e.g., '`elastic` (default)' — to match the table style used in the example page and avoid the Yes/No column.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.

Decision support is present throughout: each opt-in feature (provisioned throughput, backups) covers when to enable, when the default is fine, and the cost tradeoff. Meets the guide-not-config-reference bar.
The 'When NOT to use' cost comparison ($0.30/GB vs $0.023/GB) is concrete and source-grounded. Engineers making an EFS vs S3 choice will immediately see the 10x cost difference.

FAQ spans all three required categories — Stacktape-specific (how to mount, throughput choice), AWS use-case (cost, backup, limits), and comparison (EFS vs S3, EFS vs EBS). All 10 Q&As are substantive, not filler.

Strict compliance with example page structure: noun-form H2s, same MDX component palette (Warning + Tip), ApiReference at the bottom before FAQ, frontmatter keys match. Section-to-section consistency is maintained.
problems: The volumeMounts code example uses a discriminated union shape `{ type: 'efs', properties: { efsFilesystemName, mountPath } }` and the prose table lists a `rootDirectory` property — but the `ContainerEfsMount` interface is not included in the provided source files. These property names and the `type` discriminant cannot be verified from the sources given. If the actual API shape differs, the code example directly misleads a production engineer.

The FAQ answer on Infrequent Access storage (~$0.016/GB/month) is not in the provided source files. It's a well-known AWS fact but is stated with the same authority as sourced figures. Since the page otherwise carefully sources prices, this breaks the implicit contract with the reader.
optionalImprovements: Verify the ContainerEfsMount type shape — specifically the `type: 'efs'` discriminant, `efsFilesystemName`, `mountPath`, and `rootDirectory` property names — against the actual container workload types source (not included in the provided source files) before publishing. Add a one-sentence networking note in the Mounting section: EFS mount targets must be reachable from the container's VPC — Stacktape places both EFS and container workloads in the same stack VPC automatically, but engineers reusing a VPC across stacks should know this constraint exists.

The Backups section mentions 'storage costs stay proportional to the rate of change' without naming the billing unit (per GB/month of backup storage). One sentence naming the unit would help production engineers estimate backup costs before enabling the flag.

The FAQ answer for 'How much does Amazon EFS cost?' states ~$0.016/GB/month for Infrequent Access. This figure is not in the provided source files. Either drop the specific number and replace with 'significantly cheaper' or verify the current AWS pricing and mark it as approximate.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

The 'Mounting to containers' section is the page's strongest section: property table for volumeMounts entries, realistic multi-resource code example covering both EfsFilesystem and WebService, explicit callout about multi-replica shared-write semantics, and rootDirectory coverage for workload isolation.

issues.statement: Lambda functions do not expose `volumeMounts` in Stacktape's configuration. If you need shared persistent storage accessible from a Lambda function, use an S3 Bucket instead.
issues.reason: Lambda functions DO support EFS volume mounts in Stacktape. The `LambdaFunctionProps` interface includes `volumeMounts?: LambdaEfsMount[]` with a dedicated `LambdaEfsMount` type. The mount path must start with `/mnt/` and `joinDefaultVpc: true` is required. This FAQ answer actively misleads readers into thinking EFS+Lambda is impossible in Stacktape when it is a supported feature.
issues.suggestedFix: Rewrite the FAQ answer to explain that Lambda functions DO support EFS via `volumeMounts`, note the `joinDefaultVpc: true` requirement and the `/mnt/` mount path constraint, and mention the tradeoff (Lambda loses direct internet access when in VPC). Also update the 'Mounting to containers' section to add Lambda functions to the list of workload types that support EFS volume mounts.

evidence.quote: volumeMounts?: LambdaEfsMount[];
Required when using `volumeMounts` (EFS).

issues.statement: Container workload types that support EFS volume mounts: Web service, Private service, Worker service, Multi-container workload
issues.reason: The 'Mounting to containers' section lists only four container workload types as supporting EFS mounts, omitting Lambda functions entirely. Since `LambdaFunctionProps` has `volumeMounts?: LambdaEfsMount[]`, the enumeration is incomplete. Lambda is a major Stacktape compute type and readers configuring Lambda+EFS would be misled by this incomplete list. Additionally, the section title 'Mounting to containers' further implies EFS is container-only when Lambda support exists.
issues.suggestedFix: Rename the section to 'Mounting to workloads' or similar. Add Lambda function to the list with a note about requirements: `joinDefaultVpc: true` is required, mount paths must start with `/mnt/`, and the function loses direct internet access when in VPC. Consider showing a brief Lambda+EFS code example or at minimum linking to the Lambda function page.

issues.statement: Use `$ResourceParam` to reference the EFS filesystem's ARN in other parts of your config — for example, in a custom IAM policy statement or a CloudFormation override.
issues.reason: The type `EfsFilesystemReferencableParam = 'arn'` exists in `efs-filesystem.d.ts`, but it is NOT included in the `StacktapeResourceReferenceableParam` union in `__helpers.d.ts`. All other resource types with referenceable params are included in this union (ApplicationLoadBalancer, Bucket, Function, etc.). This may indicate the feature is not fully wired up, which could mean the `<ReferenceableParams>` component renders empty or `$ResourceParam` doesn't resolve for EFS.
issues.suggestedFix: Verify that `$ResourceParam('myEfs', 'arn')` actually works at deploy time. If it does, the section is fine (the missing union entry is a type-level oversight). If it doesn't work, remove the referenceable params section and suggest using overrides or `Fn::GetAtt` as alternatives. Either way, word the section cautiously until confirmed.

issues.statement: The 'Mounting to containers' section shows only a WebService example with no mention of Lambda-specific constraints or differences.
issues.reason: Lambda EFS mounts have different constraints compared to container workload mounts: (1) mount paths must start with `/mnt/` (container mounts have no such restriction), (2) `joinDefaultVpc: true` is required on the Lambda, (3) the Lambda loses direct internet access when joined to the VPC. The section should provide per-variant equal depth, showing at minimum the Lambda constraints alongside the container example — this is a union-type coverage gap across two distinct mount interfaces (ContainerEfsMount vs LambdaEfsMount).
issues.suggestedFix: Add a subsection or callout explaining Lambda EFS mount differences: mountPath must start with `/mnt/`, `joinDefaultVpc: true` is required, and the tradeoff that VPC-joined Lambdas lose direct internet access. Include a brief code example showing a LambdaFunction with volumeMounts.

verifierResults.summary: The EFS Filesystem draft is well-structured and mostly source-grounded, but contains one high-severity factual error: it claims Lambda functions do not support EFS volume mounts in Stacktape, when in fact `LambdaFunctionProps` has `volumeMounts?: LambdaEfsMount[]` with a dedicated type. This error appears in both the FAQ and the workload enumeration section. Lambda EFS mounts have unique constraints (mount path must start with `/mnt/`, `joinDefaultVpc: true` required, VPC removes direct internet access) that the page should document. The `StacktapeResourceReferenceableParam` union does not include `EfsFilesystemReferencableParam`, raising a question about whether `$ResourceParam` actually works for EFS — this should be verified before publication. All other claims — throughput modes, defaults, property names, backup behavior, pricing, cross-links, and code examples — are correctly grounded in the source.

The draft is broadly aligned with the EFS source type and uses the required CodeBlock/API reference patterns, but it contains several unsupported Stacktape-specific and pricing/configuration claims that should be corrected before publication.

issues.statement: “To enable at-rest encryption, use overrides to set the `Encrypted` property on the underlying CloudFormation `AWS::EFS::FileSystem` resource.”
issues.reason: The provided Stacktape EFS type exposes `backupEnabled`, `throughputMode`, and `provisionedThroughputInMibps`, plus generic `overrides`, but the source does not identify the generated EFS CloudFormation logical resource, confirm an `AWS::EFS::FileSystem` child, or document that setting `Encrypted` through overrides is a supported Stacktape path. This is a Stacktape-specific escape-hatch instruction that would change configuration.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the claim: say that `EfsFilesystemProps` does not expose a dedicated at-rest encryption toggle. If users need properties not exposed by Stacktape, point generally to overrides without naming a specific child resource or property unless sourced.

issues.statement: “Infrequent Access storage (configurable via overrides) can reduce costs to ~$0.016/GB/month for rarely accessed files.”
issues.reason: The provided source only gives one concrete EFS price: approximately $0.30/GB/month for standard access. reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
